response:
**Results**

We evaluated ten experimental variants of the proposed method on the validation set, reporting mean accuracy and standard deviation over five random seeds for each configuration (Table 1). Among these, *variant_03* serves as our baseline, which omits the proposed component, achieving a validation accuracy of 67.8 ± 0.6%. 

The majority of the proposed variants exhibited lower performance relative to the baseline. Specifically, *variant_01* (67.2 ± 0.8%), *variant_02* (66.9 ± 0.7%), *variant_04* (65.1 ± 0.9%), *variant_05* (66.3 ± 1.0%), *variant_06* (64.7 ± 0.7%), *variant_07* (65.8 ± 0.8%), *variant_09* (66.5 ± 0.7%), and *variant_10* (65.9 ± 0.8%) all underperformed when compared to the baseline, with absolute accuracy differences ranging from –0.6% to –3.1%. These results suggest that, in most configurations, the integration of the proposed component did not lead to improved validation accuracy and, in some cases, had a detrimental effect.

Notably, *variant_08* was the only configuration to outperform the baseline, achieving a validation accuracy of 70.4 ± 0.5%, which corresponds to an absolute improvement of +2.6% over the baseline. This result demonstrates that, while most instantiations of the proposed method do not enhance model performance, a specific configuration (*variant_08*) yields a substantial and consistent gain in validation accuracy. 

In summary, these findings indicate that the effectiveness of the proposed component is highly dependent on its configuration. Only one out of nine tested variants surpassed the baseline, underscoring the importance of careful design and hyperparameter selection when integrating the proposed method.
